era_context: Identical to -1055: Late Bronze Age aftermath, Iron Age consolidation. Egypt under Ramesses XI (20th Dynasty); Zhou Dynasty in early Western Zhou period. This is a time of state reorganization across Eurasia following the Bronze Age collapse disruptions of the 12th century BCE.

events.title: Egyptian-Libyan interactions during late 20th Dynasty
events.region: Egypt and Libya (Mediterranean coast and Delta)
events.description: During the reign of Ramesses XI, Libyan groups (Libu, Meshwesh, and other Sea Peoples-related populations) were increasingly settling in the Nile Delta and Lower Egypt. These groups had been raiding Egypt for generations but were now being incorporated into Egyptian society, military, and administration. This process of Libyan integration would accelerate after the 20th Dynasty and eventually lead to Libyan dynasties ruling Egypt (21st–22nd Dynasties). Contemporary evidence comes from temple reliefs and administrative records.

sources.name: Temple reliefs at Medinet Habu depicting Libyan prisoners and settlers
Administrative papyri mentioning Libyan troops and settlements

events.certainty_note: The process of Libyan settlement is well-attested archaeologically and epigraphically, but specific year-by-year events are not documented. This entry reflects a broader trend occurring during Ramesses XI's reign.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are documented for 1056 BCE. The entry above describes a long-term process rather than a discrete historical event. The significance of this year is entirely conventional and reflects the broader era rather than any documented occurrence.
historiographic_note: 1056 BCE has no documented events in surviving sources. The historical record for this year is essentially null; what is provided is era context. This illustrates a fundamental limitation of year-by-year historical chronology for the ancient world: most years leave no trace in the surviving record. The bias toward elite, literate, monumental cultures (Egypt, China) means that vast regions and populations are invisible.
